flashcards.front: What is the basic principle of Selection Sort?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort repeatedly selects the minimum element from the unsorted portion and places it at the beginning of the sorted portion.

flashcards.front: What is the basic principle of Bubble Sort?
flashcards.back: Bubble Sort compares adjacent elements and swaps them if they are in the wrong order, causing larger elements to 'bubble up' to the end.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Selection Sort?
flashcards.back: Time complexity is O(n²) for all cases (best, average, worst) due to nested loops for comparisons.

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Bubble Sort?
flashcards.back: Best case O(n) when array is already sorted, average and worst case O(n²).

flashcards.front: What is the space complexity of both Selection Sort and Bubble Sort?
flashcards.back: Both have O(1) space complexity as they sort in-place using only a constant amount of extra memory.

flashcards.front: Is Selection Sort stable or unstable?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort is unstable because swapping may change the relative order of equal elements.

flashcards.front: Is Bubble Sort stable or unstable?
flashcards.back: Bubble Sort is stable when implemented carefully (only swap on strictly greater/less condition).

flashcards.front: How many swaps does Selection Sort perform?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort performs exactly n-1 swaps (one swap per pass), making it good for memory writes.

flashcards.front: What is the key advantage of Selection Sort?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort makes minimum number of swaps (O(n)), ideal when memory writes are expensive.

flashcards.front: What optimization can improve Bubble Sort?
flashcards.back: Adding a flag to check if any swaps occurred; if no swaps, the array is already sorted and algorithm can terminate early.

flashcards.front: How many passes does Selection Sort require?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort requires n-1 passes to completely sort an array of n elements.

flashcards.front: What is the main difference in inner loop behavior between the two algorithms?
flashcards.back: Selection Sort searches for minimum in remaining unsorted portion; Bubble Sort compares adjacent pairs repeatedly.

flashcards.front: When is Bubble Sort most efficient?
flashcards.back: Bubble Sort is most efficient O(n) when the input array is already sorted or nearly sorted.

flashcards.front: Why are Selection and Bubble Sort considered simple but inefficient?
flashcards.back: They use simple logic with O(n²) time complexity, making them unsuitable for large datasets.

flashcards.front: What adaptive property does Bubble Sort have that Selection Sort lacks?
flashcards.back: Bubble Sort can detect sorted arrays early (best case O(n)), while Selection Sort always takes O(n²).
